title: Controlled Preview Boundary Pack
context: GrantLayer is publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/Discodone/grantlayer.git in a Developer Preview / controlled-pilot posture. GL-187 through GL-197 delivered a clean public snapshot, deterministic examples, feedback infrastructure, safety/claim gate, smoke matrix, and API/SDK/agent value decision. GL-197 concluded api_first_agent_examples_now_sdk_later and identified that the controlled preview boundary had not been formally documented. GL-198 creates that boundary pack as a review/docs/test artifact only.
controlled_preview_purpose: A narrow, bounded technical preview for qualified external participants to evaluate the core value proposition using publicly available examples and documentation, provide technical feedback on the API design and developer experience, and run deterministic no-install examples to gain confidence in the public snapshot. Not a production SaaS trial, not a multi-tenant pilot with real customer data, not a validator of tenant/workspace isolation, not an official SDK distribution, and not a production SLA commitment.
controlled_preview_audience: Technically qualified external participants who understand they are interacting with a Developer Preview, not a production-ready SaaS product. All participants must understand the synthetic-data-only constraint, agree not to submit real customer data or private grants, route security-sensitive reports through GitHub Security Advisories, and not treat examples as a production SDK.
allowed_participant_profiles: grant/compliance domain reviewer using synthetic scenarios only
security-minded reviewer using high-level public-safe reporting only
product/UX reviewer for docs and examples
disallowed_participant_profiles: real customer pilot with private data
security exploit publication in public issue
integration requiring secrets or private credentials
allowed_data: public GitHub issue feedback without secrets
high-level security concern without exploit details
allowed_activities: inspect API docs and server path documentation
provide documentation and DX feedback via GitHub Issues (no secrets, no exploit details)
ask product questions with public/synthetic context
start the local backend with synthetic data on a local machine with placeholder tokens
report security-sensitive issues via GitHub Security Advisories
inspect sdk/python/README.md and sdk/python/grantlayer_client.py as a local reference only
forbidden_activities: processing real grant or customer data
testing tenant isolation with real tenants
uploading secrets, private grant data, or PII
treating examples as a production SDK
publishing exploit details in public issues
changing GitHub visibility or repository settings
using internal/private repo paths or remotes
submitting feedback that contains real credentials
running demo endpoints with real data or non-local binding without explicit acknowledgement
allowed_environments: local developer machine with synthetic/demo data
local CI/test pipeline with synthetic data
forbidden_environments: public-facing server with demo endpoints enabled without explicit acknowledgement

constraints: Demo endpoints are disabled by default (GRANTLAYER_ENABLE_DEMO_ENDPOINTS=false)
When demo endpoints are enabled, they must be bound to a local host (localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1) or empty bind address
Binding demo endpoints to a non-local interface is blocked at startup unless GRANTLAYER_ALLOW_PUBLIC_DEMO_ENDPOINTS=true is explicitly set
Controlled preview participants must not expose demo endpoints publicly without understanding this guard
Demo endpoints must not be used with real grant data, real customer records, or real credentials
API-first: README table, docs/openapi.yaml, and docs/ten_minute_quickstart.md are sufficient for Developer Preview evaluation
Examples now: the two deterministic no-install examples are the highest-value public assets
SDK later: no official SDK or pip package is claimed; sdk/python/grantlayer_client.py is a local reference only
Agent workflows are examples/guidance, not production automation
No SDK implementation added in GL-198

scope: potential injection or API abuse patterns
public_issues_allowed_for: general design questions without exploit details
high-level security observations without bypass instructions

forbidden_claims: The Python SDK is available on PyPI
GrantLayer is production-ready for institutional use
GrantLayer guarantees audit trail immutability in production
Reviewer data is tenant-isolated from other reviewer data
allowed_claims: GrantLayer is an API-first Developer Preview
The examples run deterministically with no install and no network
The public snapshot is free of internal paths and real secrets
Security-sensitive reports go to GitHub Security Advisories
No pip package is published; the local wrapper is a reference example

preview_entry_criteria: GL-191 through GL-197 public docs published
first output helper works (MATCH result confirmed)
grant lifecycle example works (DIFF CLEAN result confirmed)
feedback infrastructure exists (GitHub Issues + Security Advisories)
no current blocker from GL-195, GL-196, or GL-197
demo endpoint safety guard in place (GL-190)
preview_exit_criteria: clear reviewer feedback captured and triaged
no private data or security leak identified
no misleading production or tenant isolation claims
known follow-ups converted into roadmap issues
production-readiness gaps explicitly deferred to GL-199
decision made on whether to widen the preview, proceed to pilot, or continue boundary
escalation_criteria: real customer data, PII, or private grants found in the public snapshot
real secret, token, or private key found in the public snapshot
security-sensitive finding posted publicly with exploit details
demo endpoint exposed to the public internet with real data
evidence of unauthorized production deployment claiming GrantLayer identity
internal hostname, private remote, or internal path found in public snapshot
decision_rationale: Input review from GL-187 through GL-197 confirms: clean public snapshot (no internal paths, no real secrets), deterministic examples verified (MATCH and DIFF CLEAN), safety/claim gate passed with cautions (GL-195), smoke matrix passed (GL-196, 22 check IDs), API/SDK/agent decision clear (GL-197), security-sensitive reporting routing established (GL-192), demo endpoint safety guard in place (GL-190). No finding from GL-195, GL-196, or GL-197 is blocking. A narrow controlled preview is acceptable for public docs/examples/API-understanding feedback under strict data and participant boundaries. The preview must use only synthetic/demo data and must not involve production deployments, real customer data, private grants, secrets, tenant-isolation claims, or official SDK/package claims.

findings.summary: The controlled preview boundary has not been formally documented until this issue. Reviewers joining the preview before GL-198 are operating without an explicit data safety boundary document.
findings.evidence: GL-197 risk: 'The controlled preview boundary (GL-198) has not been formally documented; until it is, the reviewer pool should remain limited.'
findings.recommended_action: Publish GL-198 boundary pack. Ensure all future reviewer invitations reference this document.
findings.recommended_issue: GL-198 (this issue); GL-198P for public publish

findings.summary: The README Developer entry path table labels Step 5 as 'Python SDK' without a qualifier, which may set incorrect expectations about published package availability.
findings.evidence: GL-197-F002: README.md Developer entry path table, Step 5: 'Python SDK' — sdk/python/README.md. The SDK README correctly states 'No pip package is published,' but the table label alone is ambiguous.
findings.recommended_action: Add a qualifier to the README table: change 'Python SDK' to 'Minimal Python client (local only, not published)' or equivalent.
findings.recommended_issue: GL-198P or a dedicated README fix issue

findings.summary: llms.txt Next Steps section still references GL-193 as an upcoming issue, but GL-193 is complete. First identified in GL-195-F003, carried through GL-197-F003.
findings.evidence: llms.txt references GL-193 as upcoming. GL-193 was merged and published.
findings.recommended_action: Update llms.txt Next Steps to reference GL-198 to GL-199 roadmap.
findings.recommended_issue: GL-198P or a small docs refresh

findings.summary: No consolidated reminder of the tenant-isolation-not-implemented constraint appears in the ten-minute quickstart or agent quickstart entry paths.
findings.evidence: docs/ten_minute_quickstart.md and docs/agent_quickstart.md focus on the happy path without a prominent tenant isolation caveat. The constraint is documented in README.md and SECURITY.md but not surfaced at the quickstart entry point.
findings.recommended_action: Add a one-line Developer Preview caveat to the top of docs/ten_minute_quickstart.md and docs/agent_quickstart.md referencing the boundary doc or SECURITY.md.

findings.summary: No agent-to-API integration example exists showing a coding agent calling the live local backend (health → create grant → demo action → check audit) with placeholder tokens.
findings.evidence: GL-197-F004: examples/langgraph_langchain/grantlayer_agent_example.py exists but is not a standalone, fully documented agent-to-API smoke path.
findings.recommended_action: Add a future standalone agent-to-API example that uses placeholder tokens and produces a structured JSON report.
findings.recommended_issue: GL-199 or a future agent example issue

findings.summary: No consolidated production readiness gap report v2 exists. Individual caveats are spread across README.md, SECURITY.md, and docs/security_boundaries.md.
findings.evidence: GL-194-F002, GL-195-F004, GL-197-F007 all identify this gap.
findings.recommended_action: GL-199 Production Readiness Gap Report v2 should consolidate all remaining hardening gates.

findings.summary: No formal reviewer data safety checklist exists for participants who wish to run the backend with synthetic-but-realistic data from real organizations.
findings.evidence: The boundary doc defines the data boundary. No formal per-reviewer checklist exists for edge cases such as a grant reviewer who works at a real organization but wants to test with their own anonymized scenarios.
findings.recommended_action: Consider a pilot data safety checklist follow-up if any reviewer proposes using organization-specific but anonymized scenarios. Defer until a concrete request arises.
findings.recommended_issue: Optional future pilot data safety checklist issue

recommended_next_issues.title: GL-198 Combined Merge-and-Publish for Controlled Preview Boundary Pack
recommended_next_issues.rationale: Merge GL-198 to main and push public snapshot update.

recommended_next_issues.title: Production Readiness Gap Report v2
recommended_next_issues.rationale: Consolidate all hardening gaps with owner, priority, and estimated scope. Covers auth, encryption at rest, IAM, SLA, tenant isolation path, and remaining gates.

optional_future_followups.notes: Capture and triage feedback from the first controlled preview participants.
Add cURL collection, expand auth docs — addresses GL-197-F001, F006.
Only after API contract stability commitment and external demand.
Complete the agent workflow integration story — GL-197-F004, GL-198-F005.
Before any pilot involving synthetic-but-realistic data from real organizations.

next_recommended_step: GL-198 Combined Merge-and-Publish for Controlled Preview Boundary Pack
non_goals: implement a real SDK or publish a pip package
change backend runtime behavior or add new API surface
change backend/src/*, OpenAPI, migrations, DB/schema, or dependency manifests
push to GitHub or change GitHub visibility
create or modify GitHub labels/issues via API
send reviewer outreach or contact reviewers
request real customer data, private grants, or secrets
